behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The validation logic alone is Unit, but 'rejected before any code is executed' is a sequencing guarantee that only appears when the validator and the module-import step are wired together in the loader. A unit test of the validator in isolation cannot prove that the loader actually gates the import behind validation. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the import call while unit-testing the validator would pass trivially without exercising the real gate. The failure boundary is between the validation module and the loader's import dispatch.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real plugin loader pointing at a temp directory containing one plugin whose manifest.json omits a required field (e.g. 'name'). Spy on / stub the Node.js module-import mechanism (e.g. require or dynamic import) to record whether it is ever called for that plugin's entry point. Run the loader. Assert: (a) the loader surfaces a validation error naming the missing field, and (b) the import spy was never invoked for that plugin.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The topological-sort algorithm is Unit-testable in isolation, but that does not prove the loader actually uses the resolved order when dispatching hook calls. The failure mode — wrong invocation order — only surfaces when the dependency resolver's output is consumed by the hook-runner. These are separate modules whose handoff is the real boundary. REAL-INFRA: No OS-level I/O is needed; in-memory or temp-dir plugin stubs suffice.
behaviors.test_description: Create two minimal plugin packages (A and B) in a temp directory. Plugin A's manifest lists B as a dependency. Each plugin's onLoad hook pushes its name onto a shared invocation log array. Run the loader. Assert the log contains ['B', 'A'] in that order.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Cycle detection is a graph algorithm testable at Unit level, but 'loads neither' requires the loader to act on the cycle-detection result — a handoff between the resolver and the hook dispatcher. The full behavior spans that module boundary. A unit test of the cycle detector proves the cycle is found but cannot prove the loader halts both plugins. REAL-INFRA: Temp-dir plugin stubs are sufficient; no real subprocess or network needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create two plugin packages (X and Y) in a temp directory where X's manifest lists Y as a dependency and Y's manifest lists X. Instrument each plugin's onLoad. Run the loader. Assert: (a) the loader returns or emits an error that identifies the cycle (mentioning both plugin names or explicitly labeling it a circular dependency), and (b) neither onLoad spy was called.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is a sequencing guarantee between the import step and the hook-invocation step inside the loader — two distinct phases that must interact correctly. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the import would hide whether real module-level code actually executes before the hook fires; the test must use a real module file whose top-level code records a side-effect. A unit test of the loader's orchestration method with a mocked importer would pass even if the ordering were reversed, because the mock has no observable execution side-effect. Real module execution is required to falsify the guarantee.
behaviors.test_description: Write a plugin entry module that, at top level (not inside onLoad), sets a flag on a shared state object (e.g. writes to a temp file or a module-level variable accessible to the test). The plugin's onLoad hook reads that flag and records whether it was already set. Load the plugin through the real loader. Assert the flag was observed as already-set inside onLoad, proving module execution preceded hook invocation.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Error-isolation logic lives inside the loader's hook-dispatch loop. The failure mode — remaining plugins silently not loaded — only appears when the loader's error-handling interacts with the multi-plugin dispatch sequence. A unit test of the error-catch block in isolation cannot verify that subsequent iterations of the dispatch loop still execute. REAL-INFRA: Temp-dir plugin stubs suffice; no OS-level I/O or network needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create three plugin packages (P1, P2, P3) with no inter-dependencies. P2's onLoad throws a synchronous error. P1 and P3 have instrumented onLoad hooks that record their invocation. Run the loader. Assert: (a) the loader surfaces an error/warning referencing P2, (b) P1's onLoad was called, and (c) P3's onLoad was called. Then add a variant where P3 declares a dependency on P2 and assert P3's onLoad is NOT called (it depends on the failed plugin).
